questions.question: What makes RNNs different from standard feedforward neural networks?

options.A: RNNs use more layers than feedforward networks
options.B: RNNs maintain a hidden state that carries information across time steps
options.C: RNNs can only process fixed-length inputs
options.D: RNNs do not use activation functions

questions.explanation: The key difference is that RNNs have a hidden state that gets updated at each time step, allowing information from previous inputs to influence the processing of current inputs. This gives RNNs a form of memory that feedforward networks lack.

questions.question: How is the hidden state in an RNN updated at each time step?

options.A: It is replaced entirely by the new input
options.B: It is computed using only the previous hidden state
options.C: It is computed using a combination of the current input and the previous hidden state
options.D: It stays the same throughout the entire sequence

questions.explanation: At each time step, the RNN combines the current input with the previous hidden state to produce a new hidden state. This combination is what allows the network to integrate new information while retaining relevant context from earlier in the sequence.

questions.question: What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs?

options.A: Gradients become too large and cause the model to diverge
options.B: Gradients shrink as they propagate back through many time steps, making it hard to learn long-range dependencies
options.C: The model runs out of memory when processing long sequences
options.D: The learning rate decreases automatically over time

questions.explanation: When training an RNN on long sequences, gradients must travel back through many time steps. At each step they get multiplied by small values, causing them to shrink exponentially. This means the network struggles to learn relationships between events that are far apart in the sequence.

questions.question: What does BPTT (Backpropagation Through Time) involve?

options.A: Training the RNN on multiple sequences simultaneously
options.B: Unrolling the RNN across time steps and applying standard backpropagation
options.C: Using a separate optimizer for each time step
options.D: Freezing the weights during the backward pass

questions.explanation: BPTT works by conceptually unrolling the RNN so each time step becomes a layer in a deep network. Standard backpropagation is then applied to this unrolled graph, allowing gradients to flow from the loss back through all the time steps to update the shared weights.

questions.question: What is the role of the forget gate in an LSTM?

options.A: It decides what new information to add to the cell state
options.B: It controls what part of the cell state to output as the hidden state
options.C: It decides what information to discard from the cell state
options.D: It resets the entire network at each time step

questions.explanation: The forget gate looks at the current input and previous hidden state and outputs values between 0 and 1 for each element in the cell state. A value close to 0 means 'forget this information' while a value close to 1 means 'keep this information.' This selective forgetting allows the LSTM to manage its long-term memory.

questions.question: What is the LSTM cell state?

options.A: A short-term buffer that resets every time step
options.B: A long-term memory that flows through the network, with gates controlling what is added or removed
options.C: The final output of the LSTM at each time step
options.D: A copy of the input sequence stored for later use

questions.explanation: The cell state acts as a conveyor belt of information flowing through the LSTM. Unlike the hidden state, the cell state can carry information across many time steps with minimal change. The forget gate, input gate, and output gate together control what gets removed from, added to, and read from the cell state.

questions.question: How does a GRU simplify the LSTM design?

options.B: It uses two gates (reset and update) instead of three, and has no separate cell state
options.C: It adds a fourth gate for extra control
options.D: It replaces the hidden state with an attention mechanism

questions.explanation: The GRU merges the LSTM's forget and input gates into a single update gate, and combines the cell state and hidden state into one. The reset gate controls how much of the previous hidden state to consider when computing new candidate values. This gives GRUs fewer parameters while often achieving comparable performance.

questions.question: Which RNN architecture type would you use for sentiment analysis of a movie review?
questions.explanation: Sentiment analysis takes a sequence of words (many inputs) and produces a single classification label such as positive or negative (one output). This makes it a many-to-one task. The RNN reads the entire review and then outputs a sentiment prediction based on the final hidden state.

questions.question: In a Seq2Seq model, what does the encoder do?

options.A: It generates the output sequence one token at a time
options.B: It processes the input sequence and compresses it into a context vector
options.C: It computes the loss function for training
options.D: It translates words one at a time without considering context

questions.explanation: The encoder reads the entire input sequence and produces a fixed-length context vector (typically its final hidden state) that summarizes the input. This context vector is then passed to the decoder, which uses it to generate the output sequence. This allows the model to handle input and output sequences of different lengths.

questions.question: What is teacher forcing?

options.A: Using a larger model to train a smaller one
options.B: Feeding the correct previous token to the decoder during training instead of its own prediction
options.C: Forcing the model to memorize the training data exactly
options.D: Increasing the learning rate to speed up training

questions.explanation: During training, teacher forcing provides the ground-truth previous token as input to the decoder at each step, rather than the token the decoder predicted. This prevents errors from compounding during training and helps the model converge faster, though it can cause a mismatch between training and inference conditions.

questions.question: During backpropagation through time, an RNN's gradients sometimes grow extremely large, producing unstable, huge weight updates. Which technique is specifically used to address this exploding-gradient problem?

options.A: Gradient clipping, which caps the gradient magnitude before the update
options.B: Adding more time steps so the gradient is spread out
options.C: Using the forget gate to delete the gradient
options.D: Switching the optimizer to plain stochastic gradient descent

questions.explanation: Exploding gradients are handled by gradient clipping, which caps the gradient's magnitude (or norm) so a single huge update cannot destabilize training. This is the counterpart to vanishing gradients, which are instead mitigated by gated architectures like LSTMs and GRUs.

questions.question: Generating a melody from a single seed note, where one input produces a whole sequence of outputs, is an example of which RNN architecture type?
questions.explanation: A single input expanding into a sequence of outputs is the one-to-many pattern, used for tasks like music or text generation from a starting seed. This contrasts with many-to-one (e.g. sentiment analysis), where a whole sequence collapses into a single output.

questions.question: In an LSTM, after the cell state has been updated, which gate decides what part of that cell state becomes the hidden state (the output passed forward at that time step)?
questions.explanation: The output gate filters the updated cell state to produce the hidden state, which is the LSTM's visible output and is also passed to the next time step. The forget and input gates manage what stays in the cell state, while the reset gate belongs to the GRU, not the LSTM.

questions.question: In a basic Seq2Seq model, the encoder must compress the entire input sequence into a single fixed-length context vector before the decoder begins. Why can this become a limitation for very long inputs?

options.A: The decoder is unable to read any input directly
options.B: The encoder and decoder are forced to share the same weights
options.C: The output sequence must always be shorter than the input
options.D: A single fixed-length vector may struggle to capture all the information in a long input

questions.explanation: Because the context vector has a fixed size, it acts as an information bottleneck: a long input can carry more detail than one vector can hold, so early information may be lost. This limitation is exactly what attention mechanisms were later introduced to relieve.

questions.question: Teacher forcing trains the decoder using ground-truth tokens, but at inference the model must feed its own (sometimes wrong) predictions back in, creating a mismatch called exposure bias. Which technique gradually shifts training from ground-truth tokens toward the model's own predictions to reduce this mismatch?
questions.explanation: Scheduled sampling starts with full teacher forcing and progressively increases the chance of feeding the model its own predictions during training. This narrows the gap between training and inference conditions, helping the model learn to recover from its own mistakes.
